title: Absence of Quantum-Metric-Induced Intrinsic Longitudinal Response
abstract: Nonlinear charge transport in solids has emerged as a powerful probe of the quantum geometric properties of Bloch electrons. While the Berry curvature underlies the intrinsic anomalous Hall effect, recent studies have suggested that the quantum metric may generate both \emph{intrinsic} nonlinear Hall and longitudinal transport. Here, using standard quantum-mechanical perturbation theory, we demonstrate that the quantum-metric-induced intrinsic longitudinal response identically vanishes, even though the corresponding intrinsic Hall response is allowed. This conclusion follows from the dissipationless nature of intrinsic currents and holds independently of band structure details and to all orders in the nonlinear response. Our work resolves existing inconsistencies in the theoretical formulation of quantum-metric-induced nonlinear transport and suggests a reexamination of recently reported intrinsic longitudinal responses attributed to the quantum metric.
summary: This paper proves that the quantum metric cannot induce an intrinsic longitudinal nonlinear conductivity in solids. It resolves theoretical discrepancies by showing that previous claims of a finite response were due to using gauge-variant energy instead of gauge-invariant energy. This suggests that reported longitudinal signals in materials like MnBi2Te4 likely stem from extrinsic, disorder-induced mechanisms.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether the quantum metric can generate an intrinsic longitudinal nonlinear transport response in solids, aiming to resolve theoretical inconsistencies in existing literature.
main_result: The author demonstrates that the quantum-metric-induced intrinsic longitudinal response identically vanishes due to the dissipationless nature of intrinsic currents, even though an intrinsic Hall response is allowed.
method: The study employs standard quantum-mechanical perturbation theory up to the second order in an external electric field, utilizing semiclassical wave-packet dynamics and the Heisenberg equation of motion.
model_or_system: The system consists of electrons in a crystal (metallic system) described by an unperturbed Hamiltonian H0 and subject to a uniform static electric field E.
key_observables: Nonlinear Hall conductivity, nonlinear longitudinal/nonreciprocal conductivity, intrinsic current density, Berry curvature, and the quantum metric.
important_parameters: Electric field strength E, relaxation time tau, chemical potential mu, and the quantum metric G and Berry curvature Omega.
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes a uniform static electric field and is truncated at the second order of the external electric field.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem and the Hamiltonian, proceeds to derive field-perturbed Bloch states and velocities using second-order perturbation theory, simplifies the resulting tensors using the quantum metric, and concludes by demonstrating the vanishing of the longitudinal response and discussing implications for experimental discrepancies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
